title: Exercise: Read a Busy Map (Sycamore Maple)
introduction:
This is a self-directed exercise on a real Darwin Tree of Life assembly of the sycamore maple (Acer pseudoplatanus), a plant. It is the most demanding of the exercises, and its lesson is judgment, not editing.

This genome loads with a busy off-diagonal checkerboard: strong contacts far from the main diagonal, in a grid-like pattern. That off-diagonal signal has more than one possible cause, and they call for opposite responses:

• Biology. Plant genomes have strong A/B compartments, which produce exactly this checkerboard. Compartments are real structure; you must not cut them.
• Assembly problem. Unpurged duplicate haplotypes, or repeat-rich sequence, also throw off-diagonal signal, and those may need attention.

Being honest: even after study, the cause here is not obvious, and telling these apart is a genuinely hard, expert call. The goal of this exercise is to practise reasoning about ambiguous signal before touching anything, not to reach a tidy answer.

Your task:
1. Recognise the busy off-diagonal and resist reacting to it.
2. Reason about whether it is biology or an assembly problem.
3. Curate only what you are confident is a real defect.

steps.title: See the busy off-diagonal
steps.instruction:
Scan the map. Unlike the other exercises, the off-diagonal here is full of structure: a checkerboard of blocks well away from the diagonal. There is still a main diagonal underneath it.

Your first instinct might be that something is badly wrong. Suspend that judgment. Busy is not the same as broken.
steps.hint: The near-flat P(s) decay and the grid pattern are both consistent with strong compartmentalisation, which is normal in plants. Do not start cutting.

steps.title: Biology or problem?
steps.instruction:
Zoom in and reason about the checkerboard:

• A/B compartments look like a plaid or checkerboard that respects the diagonal: bright squares at regular off-diagonal positions, reflecting active and inactive regions mixing at a distance.
• Duplicate haplotypes look like a bright line or block that mirrors an existing chromosome, as if a region appears twice.
• Repeats produce diffuse off-diagonal haze rather than clean squares.

Which does this look most like to you?
steps.hint: Use the Pattern Gallery to compare. If the off-diagonal pattern respects the diagonal and is regular, it is likely compartments, and compartments are biology to keep, not cut.

steps.title: Check the compartment track
steps.instruction: Look at the A/B Compartments track above the map. A strong, alternating compartment signal supports the biology interpretation. Remember that without a loaded reference FASTA the compartments are unoriented, so read the pattern, not the specific A-versus-B labels.
steps.hint: If the compartment track is strong and structured, that is evidence the checkerboard is real biology. Evidence should move you before edits do.

steps.title: Curate only clear defects
steps.instruction:
Now act, conservatively. Fix only what you are confident is a genuine assembly error:

• A clear break in the diagonal, or a butterfly inversion, is a real defect: fix it.
• A duplicated block that clearly mirrors another chromosome may be an unpurged haplotype: consider tagging it.
• The regular compartment checkerboard is biology: leave it alone.

When unsure, do nothing. Over-curating a compartmentalised genome is a classic mistake.
steps.hint: Press E for edit mode. The hard skill here is restraint: cutting real compartment structure damages the assembly. Only edit what you can defend.

steps.title: Reflect on the call you made
steps.instruction:
There is no clean answer key here, by design. Reflect:

• Did you decide the off-diagonal was mostly biology, mostly problem, or a mix?
• What evidence moved you: the pattern shape, the compartment track, the diagonal?
• Did you resist editing signal you could not confidently call a defect?

Holding uncertainty and not over-acting is exactly the judgment expert curators use on hard genomes. If you left the biology intact and fixed only clear defects, you did the job well.
steps.hint: The best outcome is a well-reasoned decision, not a perfectly clean map. Some genomes are genuinely ambiguous, and knowing that is the lesson.
